titleBlock.headline: Methionine Restriction Extends Healthspan and Lifespan in Humans: A Pilot Study
titleBlock.authors: Kuehnel SR, Diehl JO, McCarter JM, et al.

researchSummary: This is a summary of the scientific study. Not affiliated with the study authors or their institutions.
keyTakeaway: One year of methionine restriction in healthy adults is safe, feasible, and associated with broad improvements in biological aging and key measures of healthspan, many of which persisted after returning to a usual diet.
whatIsMethionine: Methionine is an essential amino acid found in high-protein foods such as meat, eggs, dairy, and nuts. It plays a key role in protein synthesis and metabolism.
Research in animals suggests that moderately reducing methionine intake can activate cellular stress responses, reduce oxidative damage, and extend lifespan.
This study is the first to evaluate the safety and effects of methionine restriction in healthy humans.

studyOverview.label: Objective
studyOverview.value: To evaluate the safety, feasibility, and preliminary effects of methionine restriction (MR) on healthspan and lifespan biomarkers in humans.

studyOverview.label: Duration
studyOverview.value: 1 year intervention with 1 year follow-up (2-year total study period).

studyOverview.label: Participants
studyOverview.value: 19 healthy adults (38-64 years; mean age ~36 years).

studyOverview.label: Intervention
studyOverview.value: Methionine-restricted diet providing ~0.31 g methionine/day (~80% reduction from standard intake), with normal protein, calories, vitamins, and minerals.

studyOverview.label: Assessments
studyOverview.value: Safety, body composition, metabolic health, physical performance, cognitive function, quality of life, and multi-omics biomarkers of aging.

control.title:
Control Diet
(Baseline)
control.desc: Standard diet: with typical methionine intake

interventionDetails.compliance: All meals provided. Compliance monitored via plasma methionine levels.

keyFindings.title: Safety & Feasibility
keyFindings.desc: MR was safe, well-tolerated, and highly compliant. No serious adverse events related to the diet.

keyFindings.title: Body Composition
keyFindings.desc: Significant reduction in total body weight, body fat, and visceral fat (p < 0.05). Lean mass preserved.

keyFindings.title: Metabolic Health
keyFindings.desc: Improvements in insulin sensitivity, LDL cholesterol, and triglycerides. Favorable changes in blood pressure.

keyFindings.title: Physical Performance
keyFindings.desc: Increases in handgrip strength and physical function measures (p < 0.05).

keyFindings.title: Cognitive Function
keyFindings.desc: Improved performance in tests of executive function and verbal memory (p < 0.05).

keyFindings.title: Quality of Life
keyFindings.desc: Improved scores in vitality, mental health, and general health perception (p < 0.05).

keyFindings.title: Biomarkers of Aging
keyFindings.desc: Favorable modulation of multi-omics aging biomarkers, including epigenetic age, transcriptomic age, proteomic age, and metabolomic age (p < 0.05).

findingsCallout: Many benefits persisted or partially persisted 1 year after returning to a usual diet.

messages: Epigenetic age decreased by ~6 years after 12 months of MR.
Significant Improvement in biological age across multiple omics layers.

conclusions: One year of methionine restriction in healthy adults is safe, feasible, and associated with improvements in multiple dimensions of healthspan, including metabolic health, physical performance, cognitive function, quality of life, and biological aging biomarkers.
Many benefits persisted after returning to a usual diet.
These findings support further long-term randomized controlled trials to determine whether MR can extend human healthspan and lifespan.
protocolRecommendations: Target methionine intake: ~0.3 g/day (~80% reduction from standard intake)
Focus on plant-forward, minimally processed whole foods
Ensure adequate intake of protein, calories, vitamins, and minerals
Monitor methionine levels and overall metabolic health
Consult a healthcare professional before making dietary changes
